title: Side Effects & Sandboxing
concept: Distinguishing read-only tools from write-active tools and sandbox strategies for the latter.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: what does an agent do when it runs a slow tool asynchronously (in the background)?

options.text: It waits frozen and can't do anything until the tool finishes
It starts the tool, keeps working, and checks back for the result in a later turn
It gives up on the tool entirely and guesses the answer

exercises.explain: Asynchronous tools run in the background. The agent doesn't sit frozen; it moves on and checks back for the result later, staying responsive while slow work happens.

cards.title: Look, Don't Touch?
cards.body: Some agent tools just look up info, like checking the weather. Others actually change the world, like sending an email you can't un-send. That difference matters a lot.

cards.title: Read vs. Write Tools
cards.body: A read-only tool only fetches information and changes nothing. A write-active tool has a side effect: it changes something real, like deleting a file or making a payment.

cards.title: Reading a Recipe vs. Cooking
cards.body: Reading a recipe is safe and repeatable, nothing happens to your kitchen. Actually cooking uses ingredients and makes a mess you can't undo. Write-active tools are the cooking part.

cards.title: Two Kinds of Tools
cards.body: 'get_weather(city)' is read-only, it just tells you the forecast. 'send_email(to, message)' is write-active, once it runs, the email is really gone.

cards.title: Myth: All Tools Are Equally Safe
cards.body: Not true! Letting an agent freely run write-active tools can cause real damage. Risky tools should run in a sandbox, a safe practice space, or need human approval first.

cards.title: Side Effect or Not?
cards.body: Read-only tools can run again and again with no harm. Write-active tools change the real world, so they need extra care.

left.text: Looks up info, changes nothing, safe to repeat

right.text: Changes something real, needs a sandbox or approval

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a WRITE-ACTIVE tool (one with a real side effect)?
exercises.explain: Deleting a user account changes something real and permanent, that's a side effect. The others only fetch information and leave the world unchanged, so they're read-only.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A sandbox lets an agent test risky write-active actions safely, without affecting the real system.
exercises.explain: True. A sandbox is a walled-off practice space. The agent can try write actions there, and mistakes stay contained instead of harming the real system.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about tool safety.
exercises.sentence: A read-only tool has no side effect, but a write-active tool should run in a sandbox for safety.
exercises.explain: A sandbox is an isolated space where a write-active tool can run without changing the real world. If something goes wrong, the damage stays contained.

exercises.prompt: Which tool description was written by an AI to sound like a safe read-only tool, even though it secretly changes things?

options.text: get_balance(): simply returns your account balance and changes nothing.
transfer_money(to, amount): moves real money out of your account.
cancel_order(id): permanently cancels the order you name.
wipe_disk(): erases all files on the drive.

exercises.explain: The AI-styled option is dressed up as harmless 'read-only' language ('simply returns... changes nothing'), which is exactly how a sneaky tool hides risk. The others are honest about their write-active side effects.

exercises.prompt: An agent has two tools: 'read_file(path)' which shows a file's contents, and 'run_command(cmd)' which can delete or modify anything. How should a careful system handle these differently?

options.text: Let both run freely with no limits, since tools are always safe
Allow read_file to run freely, but run run_command in a sandbox or require human approval
Block read_file but let run_command do anything it wants
Refuse to give the agent any tools at all

exercises.explain: read_file is read-only, it just fetches info and can't harm anything, so it's fine to run freely. run_command is write-active with big side effects, so it belongs in a sandbox or behind human approval. The key skill of this unit: recognize which tools change the world, and guard those. Great work finishing Tool Execution & Handlers!
